calculation.method: vendor_reported

calculation.method: vendor_reported

text: The UK's FTSE 100 gained 63.59 points (+0.61%) to close at 10,494.21 on the Investing.com print; Trading Economics similarly reported the blue-chip index up about 0.7%. The benchmark lagged its continental and U.S. peers because its heavy energy weighting fell alongside crude.
Italy's FTSE MIB jumped about 1.2%, the strongest performer among the major European bourses on the session.
Spain's IBEX 35 rose about 0.7% on the session.

Brent crude was down roughly 4.6% during the European session on Investing.com's reading, extending the post-truce slide in oil toward a three-month low as traders positioned for the Strait of Hormuz to reopen and Middle East supply to return.

The FTSE 100 lagged continental and U.S. peers as its heavy concentration of energy majors weighed: Shell fell about 0.5% and BP about 0.7% in tandem with retreating crude after the truce, a drag AJ Bell head of markets Dan Coatsworth flagged as the reason for the index's 'lukewarm' relative performance.

Within the DAX, financial and industrial shares led while technology and automakers lagged: GEA Group topped the index, up about 5.1% after a Deutsche Bank rating upgrade, while carmakers BMW and Mercedes-Benz fell about 5.2% and 2.8% respectively.
On the FTSE 100, defence and financial names provided support: Rolls-Royce rose about 2.7% and Babcock about 2%, while banks HSBC, Lloyds and Barclays each gained roughly 1.4% to 1.8%.

Among individual movers, STMicroelectronics fell about 2.5% after a $1.5 billion dual-tranche convertible bond offering, wealth manager Rathbones Group slumped about 17% after pausing new-client onboarding for a year, and Puma rose about 1.9% after HSBC upgraded it to Buy.

The post-truce bounce lifted Europe's year-to-date gains to nearly 8%, clawing back wartime losses and narrowing the region's valuation gap with Wall Street's S&P 500, though analysts note Europe's lighter technology weighting still caps its upside relative to the U.S. and Asia.

The Federal Reserve's official 2026 FOMC calendar lists the June 16-17 meeting, flagged for a Summary of Economic Projections, placing June 16 on the first day of a two-day policy meeting with the decision due June 17.
Investors widely expected the Federal Reserve to hold its federal funds target range at 3.50%-3.75% at the June 16-17 meeting, the first chaired by Kevin Warsh, with the decision due June 17.

text: Brent crude traded at $79.96 a barrel on the morning of June 16, 2026, its first move below $80 since the Iran war began, before edging back to about $80.19; West Texas Intermediate was down about 3.8% at $77.71.
With Iran de-escalation easing inflation fears, traders were pricing a roughly 42% chance of a 25-basis-point Fed rate hike in December, having scaled back bets on near-term tightening.
The U.S. annual CPI inflation rate stood at 4.2% for May 2026, up from 3.8% previously, per Trading Economics, underscoring the sticky-inflation backdrop the Fed faces going into the June meeting.

On June 16, 2026 the year's biggest winners led the declines - the PHLX Semiconductor Index sold off sharply (Investopedia's wrap cited a drop of nearly 6%), with Intel and AMD each down more than 6% and Nvidia off more than 2% - while industrials and financials gained and the Dow set a record, pointing to a rotation out of crowded tech rather than broad risk aversion.
